Title: Genome-wide analysis of phenylpropanoid defence pathways

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Antimicrobial activity; antioxidative activity; disease resistance; virus restriction
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Roots (for scopoletin/scopolin in Arabidopsis mentioned generally); NA for tobacco-specific accumulation site
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Tobacco scopoletin glucosyltransferase catalyses glucosylation of scopoletin. 2. Downregulation of TOGT decreased virus resistance. 3. Overexpression of TOGT increased virus resistance, supporting a positive role in coumarin-mediated defence metabolism.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic loss-of-function antisense suppression; overexpression in tobacco; metabolite analysis of scopolin/scopoletin; virus resistance assays
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This gene is not a core biosynthetic scaffold enzyme or TF; it modifies and stores the defence coumarin by glucosylation.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for engineering enhanced coumarin-based antiviral resistance by increasing scopoletin glucosylation capacity; should be coupled with upstream coumarin biosynthesis genes to ensure precursor supply.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Altered glycosylation balance may affect free scopoletin/scopolin partitioning and reactive oxygen homeostasis.

Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: midstream to downstream branch modification step
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Methylates hydroxylated lignin precursors during monolignol biosynthesis, contributing to syringyl lignin formation.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Cell wall reinforcement and pathogen defence
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Expression increased in Arabidopsis leaves after flg22, HrpZ or NPP1 treatment
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Induced during pathogen-associated molecular pattern-triggered immunity; in wheat silencing affects leaf defence
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports pathogen defence-associated lignification; silencing compromises nonhost resistance in wheat, while Arabidopsis comt1 can enhance resistance via altered soluble phenolics
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. COMT1 expression is induced during Arabidopsis PTI after flg22, HrpZ or NPP1 treatment. 2. RNAi silencing of COMT in wheat allowed penetration by the nonhost pathogen Blumeria graminis f. sp. hordei. 3. Arabidopsis comt1 mutants showed enhanced resistance to Hyaloperonospora arabidopsidis associated with accumulation of soluble 2-O-5-hydroxyferuloyl-L-malate rather than lignin changes.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Expression induction assays after elicitor treatment; RNAi silencing in wheat; mutant analysis in Arabidopsis; pathogen penetration and disease assays; metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: flg22; HrpZ; NPP1; fungal infection; Hyaloperonospora arabidopsidis; nonhost Blumeria graminis f. sp. hordei
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Phenotypic outcome is context dependent: reduced COMT can weaken nonhost resistance yet enhance resistance in Arabidopsis through rerouting to soluble phenolics.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for tuning lignin composition and defence metabolites, but deployment requires caution because reduced COMT can have opposite effects depending on species-pathogen context.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Possible altered lignin composition and mechanical properties; defence tradeoff depends on metabolite rerouting.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces cinnamoyl-CoA esters to corresponding aldehydes as the first enzyme specific to monolignol synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies direct precursors for lignin monomers
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as first enzyme specific for monolignol synthesis; key control point implied
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Cell wall reinforcement and defence signalling
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes to lignin-related pathogen defence signalling in rice
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CCR is the first enzyme specific for monolignol synthesis. 2. OsCCR1 was shown to be involved in pathogen defence signalling in rice. 3. OsCCR1 acts as an effector of OsRac1 and physically binds OsRac1 in a GTP-dependent manner.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Protein interaction analysis with OsRac1; defence signalling studies in rice
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review emphasizes its dual role as metabolic enzyme and defence signalling effector.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate to improve lignification-linked disease resistance in rice; engineering should consider its signalling interaction with OsRac1.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Potential impacts on lignin load, cell wall properties and growth if strongly manipulated.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Coumarin branch sharing intermediates with monolignol pathway
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates hydroxycinnamoyl intermediates that are required for coumarin biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Antimicrobial activity; antioxidative activity; disease resistance
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for normal scopoletin and scopolin accumulation in Arabidopsis roots
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Scopoletin and scopolin production in Arabidopsis roots requires C3H activity. 2. In C3H T-DNA insertion mutants, scopoletin and scopolin content decreased to about 3% of wild-type levels. 3. This demonstrates that C3H provides essential precursor flux into the coumarin branch.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: T-DNA insertion mutant analysis; root metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although widely known in monolignol biosynthesis, the review specifically highlights its essential role in coumarin production.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful upstream target to elevate coumarins, but pathway engineering must balance effects on lignin and other phenylpropanoids.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Because C3H sits in shared phenylpropanoid metabolism, manipulation may broadly alter lignin and related phenolics.

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Oryza sativa; Glycine max; Medicago truncatula; others
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Arabidopsis; Rice; Soybean; Medicago; multiple plants
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Oryza sativa; Glycine max; Medicago truncatula
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: EM (Embrapa-48); PI230970 for soybean expression comparison
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses one coumaroyl-CoA with malonyl-CoA units through decarboxylation, condensation and cyclization to form chalcone, the first committed flavonoid intermediate.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Antimicrobial defence; UV protection; signalling in plant-microbe interactions
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Gene family expanded especially in legumes
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Arabidopsis CHS reduced in most infection experiments; soybean and Medicago CHS family members strongly induced by pathogens/elicitors; rice CHS slightly induced by Magnaporthe infection
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Controls entry into flavonoid/isoflavonoid biosynthesis and pathogen-responsive flavonoid accumulation, especially in legumes
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CHS is the first committed enzyme of flavonoid biosynthesis. 2. CHS gene families are expanded in legumes, consistent with diversification of defence flavonoids and isoflavonoids. 3. Many soybean and Medicago CHS genes are induced by pathogens or elicitors, and in soybean several CHSs coexpress with isoflavonoid genes, suggesting specialized roles in defence metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical enzyme characterization; structural biology; phylogenetic analysis; microarray expression profiling under pathogen/elicitor treatments
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Cyst nematode infection; Asian soybean rust; Phymatotrichopsis omnivora; yeast elicitor; methyl jasmonate; Magnaporthe oryzae; Pseudomonas; Phytophthora
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Affymetrix microarray data from AtGenExpress; Expression Profiler; MtGEAv2; accession E-MEXP-808; E-TABM-230
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review emphasizes CHS as a core summarized pathway gene rather than one single locus. A Phe215Ser point mutation can shift substrate preference dramatically.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong engineering node for elevating flavonoid/isoflavonoid defence chemistry, especially by selecting inducible legume CHS paralogues for pathway stacking.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Manipulation can redirect carbon into flavonoids at expense of other phenylpropanoid branches and may alter pigmentation or growth.

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Lotus japonicus; Medicago truncatula; Glycine max; others
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces a CHS-derived unstable intermediate to direct pathway flux into 5-deoxyflavonoid formation.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Described as critical enzyme for the 5-deoxyflavonoid pathway
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: True CHR cluster restricted to legume enzymes
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Three CHRs induced in soybean during pathogen response
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes 5-deoxyflavonoid and downstream isoflavonoid phytoalexin biosynthesis in legumes
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CHR is the critical enzyme for the 5-deoxyflavonoid pathway. 2. CHR genes expanded in legumes and only legume enzymes group in the true CHR cluster. 3. Soybean CHR genes are induced together with isoflavonoid defence genes during pathogen responses.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical characterization of CHR function; phylogenetic analysis; pathogen-responsive microarray profiling
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Cyst nematode infection; Asian soybean rust
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Affymetrix microarray data including E-MEXP-808 and E-TABM-230
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Nonlegume CHR-like genes exist but their functions are unclear.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Key target to channel flavonoid flux toward antimicrobial 5-deoxy compounds in legumes; best used with CHS/CHI/IFS stacking.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May reduce competing flavonoid branches and alter constitutive phenolic balance.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Glycine max; Medicago truncatula; Lotus japonicus
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Early flavonoid branch; type II in legume 5-deoxyflavonoids
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyses stereospecific isomerization of chalcones to corresponding flavanones.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor for downstream flavonoids/isoflavonoids
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Type I and II CHI gene clusters occur in legumes
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: CHI genes induced in soybean pathogen responses
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports production of flavanone precursors for defence flavonoids and legume-specific 5-deoxyflavonoids
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CHI catalyses stereospecific chalcone-to-flavanone conversion. 2. Type II CHIs in legumes accept both deoxy- and hydroxychalcones and underpin 5-deoxyflavonoid biosynthesis. 3. CHI genes are expanded and clustered in legumes and induced during soybean defence responses.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical characterization; phylogenetic analysis; pathogen-responsive expression profiling
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Type II CHIs are considered a key evolutionary innovation associated with Fabaceae.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful branch-selective target for enhancing legume defence isoflavonoids, especially when co-engineered with CHR and IFS.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Could alter balance among flavonoid subclasses.

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Glycyrrhiza echinata; Glycine max; Medicago truncatula; Lotus japonicus; others
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Glycine max; Medicago truncatula
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyses 2-hydroxylation of flavanone followed by 1,2-aryl migration to generate 2-hydroxyisoflavanone, the entry reaction to isoflavone biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms immediate precursor to isoflavones
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Described as entry enzyme into isoflavonoid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Antimicrobial defence; signalling in plant-microbe interactions
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Two IFS genes induced in soybean pathogen responses; IFS-like gene induced in Medicago by fungal infection/elicitors
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enables isoflavonoid branch formation and defence phytoalexin production
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. IFS catalyses the entry reaction into isoflavonoid biosynthesis. 2. Catalytically critical active-site residues, including Lys375, determine the unique aryl-migration reaction. 3. IFS genes are present in low copy number in legumes and are induced in pathogen-responsive soybean and Medicago datasets; Arabidopsis expressing IFS can produce isoflavones even without HID.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Enzyme characterization; site-directed mutagenesis; heterologous expression in Arabidopsis; expression profiling
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Pathogen infection; fungal elicitors; methyl jasmonate; yeast elicitor
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Affymetrix soybean and Medicago microarray data
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review highlights IFS as a hallmark legume innovation; Arabidopsis heterologous expression proves sufficiency for isoflavone formation under some contexts.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Prime engineering gene to install isoflavonoid biosynthesis into nonlegumes or strengthen phytoalexin production in legumes; likely most effective with HID/I2'H/IFR/OMT stacking.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May divert flavanone flux from anthocyanin/flavonol branches and produce novel metabolites with pleiotropic effects.

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Glycyrrhiza echinata or Glycyrrhiza uralensis (licorice not specified); Glycine max
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyses dehydration of 2-hydroxyisoflavanones to produce isoflavones.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Critical in Lotus hairy roots; dispensable in Arabidopsis expressing IFS
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes efficient isoflavone formation in some legumes
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. The dehydration from 2-hydroxyisoflavanone to isoflavone is enzyme-catalysed rather than spontaneous. 2. HID from licorice and soybean shows substrate specificities matching species-specific isoflavone patterns. 3. HID appears critical for isoflavone biosynthesis in Lotus japonicus hairy roots, but Arabidopsis expressing IFS forms isoflavones without HID.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Enzyme characterization; site-directed mutagenesis/phylogeny; hairy root functional analysis; heterologous expression comparison
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Requirement for HID is species/context dependent.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Recommended as an auxiliary engineering gene to improve isoflavone yield/stability in heterologous systems where spontaneous dehydration is limiting.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Likely modest; may influence product spectrum among isoflavone types.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates the B-ring of isoflavones at the 2' and/or 3' position, enabling downstream pterocarpan or coumestrol biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor for downstream defence phytoalexins
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Unique tissue-specific expression patterns in Medicago CYP81E enzymes
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Medtr CYP81E9 induced in response to symbiotic mycorrhizal fungus; soybean I2'H genes induced in pathogen response
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Provides essential hydroxylation chemistry for defence isoflavonoid diversification
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. I2'H (CYP81E1) was first cloned from licorice. 2. Medicago CYP81E enzymes produce different 2' and/or 3' hydroxylated products and show distinct tissue-specific expression, implying specialization in defence processes. 3. I2'H genes are induced in soybean pathogen responses and one Medicago member is induced during mycorrhizal colonization.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Cloning and enzyme characterization in yeast; phylogenetic analysis; expression profiling
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Microarray data for Medicago and soybean
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Different CYP81E paralogues may partition roles between defence and symbiosis-related flavonoid metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Important tailoring gene for producing specific pterocarpan/coumestrol products; should be selected according to desired hydroxylation pattern and tissue context.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Can change isoflavonoid spectrum and potentially affect symbiosis-related signalling.

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Various legumes including Medicago sativa and Glycine max
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyses NADPH-dependent reduction of 2'-hydroxyformononetin to (3R)-vestitone in pterocarpan phytoalexin biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor for downstream pterocarpans
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Described as key enzyme in latter pathway
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Multiple soybean IFRs induced under pathogen response conditions
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. IFR is a key enzyme in the latter part of the pterocarpan phytoalexin pathway. 2. It reduces 2'-hydroxyformononetin to (3R)-vestitone using NADPH. 3. Structural work defined the substrate/cofactor-binding cleft, and multiple soybean IFR genes are pathogen inducible.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Enzyme assays; protein crystallography; pathogen-responsive expression profiling
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Many IFR-like genes exist, so biochemical proof is important for annotation.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong candidate for boosting pterocarpan phytoalexins, especially together with IFS, I2'H, VR and OMTs.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May deplete upstream isoflavone pools and alter constitutive flavonoid composition.

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Pisum sativum; Medicago sativa; Medicago truncatula; Cicer arietinum
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Entry methylation step toward methoxy isoflavonoid phytoalexins
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: 4'-O-methylated isoflavanone intermediates leading to formononetin-derived phytoalexins
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers a methyl group from SAM to the B-ring 4'-hydroxyl of 2-hydroxyisoflavanone intermediates.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor for target phytoalexins
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: methoxy isoflavonoid phytoalexins including medicarpin/pisatin precursors
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes methoxylated isoflavonoid phytoalexin biosynthesis in several legumes
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HI4'OMT catalyses a critical entry-point 4'-O-methylation for isoflavonoid phytoalexin formation in pea, alfalfa, M. truncatula and chickpea. 2. Medicago HI4'OMT shares high identity with pea HMM and can also methylate the unnatural pisatin intermediate 6a-hydroxymaackiain. 3. Structural studies explain how homologous enzymes catalyse distinct but related methylation reactions.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Enzyme assays with recombinant proteins; X-ray crystal structures of substrate complexes; comparative sequence analysis
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Demonstrates catalytic plasticity among legume OMTs.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Valuable tailoring enzyme for engineering species-specific methoxylated phytoalexins; homolog choice can tune substrate range.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May alter broader O-methylated phenylpropanoid spectrum if substrate promiscuity occurs.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyses stereospecific NADPH-dependent reduction of (3R)-vestitone in medicarpin biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms immediate precursor toward target product
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Controls stereospecific formation of pterocarpan phytoalexin intermediates
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. VR is the central enzyme converting nonenantiomeric isoflavones to enantiomeric pterocarpans. 2. It belongs to the SDR superfamily and is highly stereospecific for the 3R conformation of vestitone. 3. Homologues occur in multiple species, emphasizing conserved late-step control of phytoalexin stereochemistry.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical characterization; structural and substrate-specificity analysis
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review emphasizes stereochemical specificity as a determinant of phytoalexin product identity.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Important for accurate reconstitution of pterocarpan pathways in metabolic engineering, especially where stereochemistry affects bioactivity.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Likely minimal outside pathway flux redistribution.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Terminal methylation step in pisatin branch
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Methylates 6a-hydroxymaackiain to form the phytoalexin pisatin.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Hairy roots/tissues under infection context; NA for specific organ
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for pisatin accumulation and pea resistance to Nectria haematococca
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HMM catalyses the last enzyme step in pisatin biosynthesis. 2. Antisense HMM expression in pea hairy roots strongly reduced pisatin content. 3. Reduced pisatin led to increased susceptibility to Nectria haematococca, genetically supporting the defence role of pisatin.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Antisense suppression in pea hairy roots; metabolite quantification; fungal susceptibility assay
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Two genetically linked HMMs suggest duplication associated with pisatin biosynthesis specialization.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Excellent terminal gene for engineering/strengthening pisatin-based antifungal resistance in pea or for pathway reconstruction in heterologous legumes.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Could increase allocation to phytoalexins and alter methyltransferase substrate competition.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces sophorol with stereochemical preference to produce the immediate intermediate for pisatin formation.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor for target product
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports late stereoselective steps in pisatin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SOR is a VR homologue identified for pisatin biosynthesis in pea. 2. It prefers (-)-sophorol over (+)-sophorol as substrate. 3. SOR catalyses one of the final three steps leading to (+)-pisatin.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Highlights stereochemical control in pisatin pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Needed for faithful reconstruction of pisatin biosynthesis where stereoselectivity matters.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Prenylation branch for sophoraflavanone G formation
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Prenylated flavanone intermediate leading to sophoraflavanone G
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers a dimethylallyl group to a flavonoid substrate during sophoraflavanone G biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enables biosynthesis of highly bioactive prenylated flavonoid sophoraflavanone G
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SfN8DT-1 was the first flavonoid-specific plant prenyltransferase cDNA isolated. 2. It is involved in sophoraflavanone G biosynthesis in Sophora flavescens. 3. Its discovery revealed evolutionary relatedness to prenyltransferases for vitamin E and plastoquinone biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: cDNA isolation; functional enzyme characterization; phylogenetic analysis
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Important example of recruitment of prenyltransferase chemistry into defence flavonoid metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Attractive gene for engineering highly antimicrobial prenylated flavonoids in medicinal or crop plants.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Prenyl donor competition and possible membrane-associated metabolic burden.

Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Prenylated pterocarpan intermediate leading to glyceollin
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyses prenylation at the pterocarpan 4-position in glyceollin biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for glyceollin biosynthesis in soybean
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. G4DT was identified after discovery of flavonoid prenyltransferases. 2. It catalyses the critical prenylation step in glyceollin biosynthesis in soybean. 3. The enzyme exemplifies late-step tailoring that enhances phytoalexin bioactivity.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Prenylation greatly enhances lipophilicity and antimicrobial activity.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong target for enhancing glyceollin accumulation and bioactivity in soybean defence engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May alter precursor availability for nonprenylated isoflavonoids.

Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Secondary wall formation and defence-related lignification
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Activates the lignin/secondary wall program including monolignol biosynthesis
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NST1 is part of the Arabidopsis transcription factor network regulating secondary wall biosynthesis. 2. NST1 together with NST3 acts as a master switch that turns on the entire secondary wall biosynthetic process, including monolignol biosynthesis. 3. It thereby exerts decisive positive control over lignin accumulation.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genetic and transcriptional network studies summarized in review
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: MYB58; MYB63; monolignol biosynthesis pathway genes
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates downstream MYB58/MYB63-mediated lignin pathway gene expression
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Monolignol biosynthesis and secondary wall deposition
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Master switch for secondary wall biosynthesis including lignin formation
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mentioned in developmental regulation rather than pathogen-specific induction.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential high-level regulator for coordinated wall/lignin engineering, but likely causes pleiotropic effects because it controls a broad secondary wall program.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Strong pleiotropy on growth, cell wall properties and biomass quality expected from master-switch manipulation.

Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Secondary wall formation and defence-related lignification
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Co-activates whole secondary wall and monolignol biosynthesis program
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NST3 acts with NST1 in the Arabidopsis secondary wall regulatory network. 2. NST3 and NST1 function as a master switch for the entire secondary wall biosynthetic process, including monolignol biosynthesis. 3. This places NST3 as a decisive positive regulator of lignin deposition.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genetic and transcriptional network studies summarized in review
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates lignin regulatory MYBs and downstream monolignol genes
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Monolignol biosynthesis and secondary wall deposition
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Master switch for lignin-associated secondary wall biosynthesis
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: High-leverage regulator for broad lignin engineering but unsuitable for subtle trait tuning without strong pleiotropic control.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Expected severe developmental and cell wall pleiotropy.

Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MYB58 is directly regulated by NST1 and NST3. 2. MYB58 can specifically activate the monolignol biosynthesis pathway. 3. It therefore acts as a more pathway-focused lignin regulator downstream of NAC master switches.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transcriptional regulatory studies summarized in review
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: PAL; C4H; COMT; CCR; CAD and related monolignol genes
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Transcriptional activation via lignin gene promoters/AC elements
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Specific activator of lignin biosynthetic pathway
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: More pathway-specific than NST1/NST3 and thus potentially more useful for targeted engineering.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising regulator for more selective lignin pathway manipulation than NAC master switches.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Still may alter wall composition and growth if strongly overexpressed.

Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MYB63 is directly regulated by NST1 and NST3. 2. MYB63 can specifically activate the monolignol biosynthesis pathway. 3. It acts downstream of NAC master regulators to promote lignin accumulation.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transcriptional regulatory studies summarized in review
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: PAL; C4H; COMT; CCR; CAD and related monolignol genes
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Transcriptional activation via lignin gene promoters/AC elements
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Specific activator of lignin biosynthesis downstream of NAC master switches
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful candidate for selective lignin engineering with potentially narrower scope than NST1/NST3.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Possible impacts on development and wall composition.
